Hirschbach Motor Lines — Expedited Division
A3 Field
Notebook
April 2026  ·  Gemba Walk
Expedited Division — Operational Assessment & Gemba Walk Documentation
01
Background Brief
Hirschbach's vision for Expedited
What Expedited is supposed to be
  • A slip-seat relay shuttle — two drivers meet in the middle, swap trucks, and each returns home the same day
  • Every driver starts and ends at their home yard. No one lives in the truck.
  • The truck never stops moving: AM and PM departure waves, two full round trips daily per truck
  • Designed to be 100% self-contained — no OTR support, no local truck feeding the shuttle, no borrowed freight
  • Financial thesis: drivers home nightly → lower turnover. Truck runs 2 shifts → 2× asset utilization vs. standard OTR
  • Long-term: relay model runs consistent routes, known lanes, fixed swap points — the exact profile needed for autonomous trucks when Aurora technology comes online
Four Distinct Operations Inside Expedited

Expedited is not a single operation. It is four distinct operations grouped under one division, each with its own freight profile, driver model, and planning discipline. When discussing Expedited performance, staffing, strategy, or financials, these areas need to be addressed separately — conflating them masks what is actually working and what is not.

CC 153
Driscoll's
Dedicated customer operation with its own freight profile and planning cadence.
Lane 153
CC 135
Aurora & Regional
Pickup & Delivery work — structurally different from relay operations. Local and regional in nature.
Lane 135
CC 147
CA – TX
Long-haul California–Texas pairing. Distinct driver and freight dynamics from the standard slip-seat model.
Lane 147
Core Slip-Seat
Standard Slip-Seat Operations
The core relay-shuttle model the Expedited thesis was built around — drivers swap in the middle and return home daily.
Lanes 148 · 149 · 154 · 151 · 156 · 152 · 155
Current Lane Structure
CC ↕ Lane ↕ Point Type ↕ HML Code ↕ Address ↕ City ↕ State ↕ Zip ↕ Team ↕ Del

G1
Gemba Observations
Sapulpa, OK — July 13–15, 2025  ·  3-day floor walk, Expedited dispatch operations
How to read this section

Observations are organized by theme, not by day. Each section shows what was seen (facts only), the operational impact, and open questions flagged for follow-up. ⚠ Q items indicate gaps, inconsistencies, or decisions that need leadership alignment before countermeasures can be designed.

Theme 1 — Strategy Alignment & Value Proposition
Theme 2 — Extra Pay
Theme 3 — Workspace, WFH, Leadership
Theme 4 — Technology
Theme 5 — HOS, Split Shifts & Disruption Recovery
Theme 6 — Communication Overload & Interruptions
Theme 7 — Shift Handoff & Accountability
Theme 8 — Driver Management, Pay & Productivity
Theme 9 — Freight Finding & Network Coverage
Theme 10 — Equipment & Trailer Operations
Theme 11 — Training, Standards & Leadership Development
Theme 12 — Support Staff Schedule & Coverage Model
T1
Strategy Alignment & Value Proposition
The gap between what Expedited is supposed to be and what it currently operates as

Expedited was launched as one thing; what operates today is something different. The gap is not in execution — the team is working hard and competently. The gap is strategic: there is no shared answer to what Expedited is or why we are doing it, and every downstream decision is being made against a different mental model. The five sub-sections below trace the drift, the two questions that have to be answered first, the three value lenses tested against reality, the reframe that holds up, and how that reframe shows up in a customer conversation.

1. The Model Has Drifted

Three potential value propositions for Expedited were evaluated against what was observed on the floor. Each was tested for whether it actually holds up under current operating conditions.

  • ~94% of orders on key cost centers depend on non-EXP trucks for pickup or delivery — most need both. Only ~6% of current freight fits the original self-contained model.
  • Driscoll's is the exception that proves the rule. At ~6% non-EXP dependency, it is the one lane today that runs essentially as the original model intended — and it works. Everything else is a variation, a workaround, or a borrowed-freight workaround on top of a workaround.
  • Expedited operates as a mix of Models A, B, and C with no single aligned strategy. The team described the network as an "octopus" — expanding in many directions simultaneously while strategy continues to evolve, with priorities shifting frequently.
  • Lanes without sufficient volume require freight pulled from other lanes or divisions to build the load. This masks the underlying demand gap, introduces inefficiency, and is structurally incompatible with the autonomous-readiness pathway.
  • The true cost of Expedited is not contained inside the Expedited cost center. Deadhead, equipment depreciation (an escalated rather than normal schedule), Expedited-specific headcount, and OOR miles all show up — directly or indirectly — in OTR or in shared overhead. Reading Expedited's OR alone understates the full operating cost of the division.
  • Network design is driving deadhead and cost. Expedited point placement, combined with where the volume actually moves, is producing increased deadhead miles and increased operating cost. The network was not designed against the freight pattern we now have.
2. Two Questions We Have to Answer First

The drift in Section 1 is the symptom. The cause is upstream — there is no shared answer to what Expedited is or why we are doing it. Until both are answered, each lane decision, each hire, and each customer conversation is being made against a different mental model.

What is Expedited?
What differentiates it from OTR, Dedicated, or Regional? Clarifying this lets us scope analysis against a defined current state and a defined future state — rather than trying to evaluate everything.
Why are we doing Expedited?
What role is Expedited intended to play for the organization?
Desired State — A Winnable Strategy
If we land both the what and the why, the rest of the work simplifies. We stop evaluating everything and start evaluating the right things — the lanes that fit, the customers worth pursuing, the investments worth making, the drivers worth recruiting.
3. Three Value Lenses, Tested Against Reality

Working backward from the why question: what value is Expedited actually delivering today? Three traditional answers — speed, driver home time, and financial OR — were tested against what was observed on the floor. Each was scored on whether it actually holds up under current operating conditions.

Value LensWhat Was ObservedHolds Up?
1. Speed
"We move freight faster"		 Speed is situational. It clearly matters when time is critical (e.g., Texas → California in 36 hours instead of 3–4 days). However, not all customers place a premium on transit speed — for many, speed is not the primary purchasing driver. Situational only
2. Driver home time
"Drivers are home daily"		 Critically important to drivers (a key reason they accept Expedited roles), but home time is not a customer purchasing decision. Customers don't pay a premium because our driver is home at night. Driver value only
3. Financial / OR
"Expedited is more profitable"		 Expedited's OR appears more favorable in part because costs are absorbed by OTR (pickup/delivery, T-call assist, borrowed freight). If all costs were properly allocated, the financial case would likely be less compelling than it currently appears. Distorted by allocation

The takeaway: all three lenses break down on contact with the operating reality. None of them sustains a durable customer story on its own. That is the cue to look for a fourth answer.

4. The Reframe & The Customer Conversation
Future-Focused Strategy
The value of Expedited is not "moving faster freight" — it is moving freight differently, with greater consistency, predictability, and future-focused capabilities. The natural customer fit is companies investing heavily in sustainable transportation technologies — Anheuser-Busch, Pepsi, Walmart suppliers, and similar. The relationship structure should look more like a dedicated contract with a sticky commitment partner than a traditional spot/expedited engagement, anchored on the crawl → walk → run path to autonomy.
  • What it implies about lanes: lanes without committed volume should be evaluated against the OTR baseline rather than kept on Expedited "to fill capacity." Lanes with committed volume + autonomous-readiness become the explicit growth target.

If the reframe is right, the selling motion changes. The pitch is no longer about transit time and rate — it is about committed volume in exchange for autonomous-readiness. Concretely, it sounds like this:

"Walmart, I know you've already bid out all of your lanes for this year. But if you can give me committed volume from Texas to Kansas City on this lane, I can start running it autonomous by the end of the year. I need you to commit to X loads per month in exchange."
  • The pitch works because it offers something the customer cannot get from a typical OTR bid — early access to autonomous capability — in exchange for the only thing Expedited needs to succeed: committed, predictable volume.
  • It also gives the customer a credible reason to revisit a closed bid mid-cycle, which is normally extremely difficult in transportation sales.
5. Expedited Freight Classification Framework — What We Want From Expedited

A three-tier classification for grading Expedited freight against the relay-shuttle model — used to diagnose where freight is controlled, where it requires active management, and where it is reactive and costly.

A
Fully Controlled / Premium ExpeditedThe Standard
Definition
Highest-priority freight fully owned by Expedited from end to end with minimal variability. The standard we aim to build the network around.
Criteria
  • Expedited controls pickup, delivery, and full planning execution
  • Load is sent directly to the Expedited division
  • No P&D involvement required
  • Drop & hook on both ends
  • Less than 75 miles variance
  • Ability to adjust to different departs 24 hours apart
  • Preplanned and stable execution plan
  • Minimal execution risk
  • Rare overweight issues
Standard — What We Want
  • Load is fully executed by EXPE in their division
  • Zero repowers or reassignments
  • Clean execution from start to finish
Examples
★ Pepsi — Wytheville, VA → Indianapolis, IN (Holy Grail)
Walmart — Colton, CA → Lancaster & Cleburne, TX
B
Managed Expedited / Controlled VariabilityWhere Discipline Matters
Definition
Freight largely controlled by Expedited that requires active management due to variability in timing, routing, execution, or customer requirements.
Criteria
  • Expedited controls at least one side (pickup or delivery)
  • P&D is needed on at least one end
  • Mixed drop & hook and live load / unload
  • Between 75–200 miles variance
  • 8–20 hours buffer
  • Moderate execution risk
Standard — What Good Looks Like
  • Load is planned with a clear direction
  • Driver is committed but may require replanning
  • Limited reworks
  • Service protected through proactive management and tracking
Examples
Walmart — Colton, CA → New Caney, TX
Walmart — Hazleton, PA → Gas City, IN
CJ Logistics — Somerset, NJ → Greenfield, IN
Walmart — Arkansas City, KS → Thomasville, GA
Walmart — Belvidere, IL → Carolinas
Sara Lee & Conagra — Indiana → East Coast
C
Reactive / High-Risk ExpeditedWhere We Lose Money & Control
Definition
Freight with high variability and limited control. Requires reactive execution and carries the highest risk to service and cost.
Criteria
  • Limited Expedited control (reactive involvement)
  • P&D active on both ends
  • Live load / live unload
  • Greater than 200 miles variance
  • Less than 8 hours buffer (or no buffer)
  • High execution risk
Reality — What to Improve
  • Load is planned multiple times
  • Driver alignment is uncertain or changing
  • Frequent intervention required
  • Focus is on recovery, not optimization
Examples
McLane — Olathe, KS → North Carolina

Coaching Language

Class AThis is what right looks like
Class BThis is where discipline matters
Class CThis is where we lose money and control

Biggest Opportunity

Turning Class B into Class A.

Reducing how much freight falls into Class C.

Extra Pay
Extra pay patterns, out-of-route cost, rate degradation, and non-self-contained operations
Extra Pay

Lane / CC BD — Breakdown DP — Detention LA — Layover MX — Mgr Pay XP — Extra Pay TOTAL

148 — IN to NC———$1,650$900$2,550
149 — IN to PA———$1,550$200$1,750
151 — TX to KS———$525$250$775
152 — IN to KS$750——$225$605$1,580

TOTAL $5,725 $350 $560 $6,383 $7,582 $20,600
Extra Pay — Clarifications & Attribution Call-Outs

Several of the line items above carry attribution caveats that need to be reflected before any conclusions are drawn. These were surfaced during the review and need to be clarified so the financial picture isn't read as Expedited overhead when it isn't.

  • Detention, Layover, and most Breakdown pay on CC 153 is Driscoll's-related, not Expedited operations. Driscoll's is structurally closest to the vest of any Expedited cost center, and the contract carries detention and layover terms that are not present on the other lanes. These should be tagged separately when evaluating Expedited's true operational extra-pay run rate.
  • Breakdown pay on CC 153 includes OTR drivers running in Expedited trucks. When an OTR driver's truck was in the shop and they were temporarily assigned an Expedited unit, any layover or breakdown pay generated during that period landed in the Expedited numbers. The cost is real, but it is not an Expedited operational decision.
  • Driscoll's incentive pay (~$7K over 15 weeks) was not part of the original lane setup. Per the team, the incentive was created when teams were placed on Driscoll's — the lane was not designed to require an additional incentive on top of base pay. This should be reviewed against the current lane economics.
  • Incentive (XP) pay is functioning as a coverage subsidy. Example: Bonnie Brooks earned ~$2,123 over the 15-week period covering Alan's vacation week — Alan was paid PTO and Bonnie was paid for working 14 consecutive days. Several similar patterns appear in the file (Brandon, Reina, others).
  • The 7-on / 7-off schedule itself is the primary hiring incentive for Expedited. It is also the structural reason XP is showing up at this volume — when someone takes time off, the only way to cover the shift today is to pay another team member to work an extra rotation. This is a policy issue, not a schedule issue.
⚠ MX (Manager Pay) — Authorization Concern
Key Context: MX (Manager Pay) should only be authorized and used by Directors. The specific use case is not fully defined. The following breakdown shows total MX pay by Director over the 15-week period. Reviewing the detail file confirms that a noticeable increase in MX usage began around early February.

The following breakdown shows total MX pay by Director over the 15-week period. Reviewing the detail file confirms that a noticeable increase in MX usage began around early February.

Director MX Total Paid Context / Notes

Kyle Ross$533CC 153 (Driscolls). One entry — "running an extra day, approved by Kyle."

Heat-map color on the YTD column groups employees by relative magnitude: red ≥ $4K, orange $2K–$4K, yellow $1K–$2K, green < $1K. The pattern is consistent with PTO usage clustered in shorter windows — large weekly spikes are typically a single coverage event rather than ongoing pay.

Out of Route
Out-of-Route Miles — Strategic Cost Signal
Strategic Context: Routing freight through Expedited instead of OTR is adding meaningful additional miles per load — an impact that will continue to grow as Expedited volume increases. We’ve identified a need to quickly review lanes and their out-of-route (OOR) miles to confirm whether this pattern is isolated or the norm.
Specific lanes we need to dive into:
  • Walmart: Colton, CA → Harrisonville, MO
  • Walmart: Colton, CA → Moberly, MO
  • Walmart: Belvidere, IL → Wellford, SC
  • Baxter Health: Marin, NC → Waukegan, IL
  • Coca-Cola: Paw Paw, MI → Mebane, NC
Example: Walmart Belvidere, IL → Wellford, SC
Route OTR Direct Expedited (4-dispatch)
Loaded Miles 754 930
Empty Miles 120 176
Total Miles 874 1,106
Extra Miles +232 miles per load (176 loaded + 56 empty)

This load has been hauled 209 times through Expedited YTD — a total of approximately 48,488 excess miles on this single lane alone. The 4-dispatch model requires 2 non-EXP trucks on the front and back end, with OTR drivers receiving higher per-mile rates on the shorter dispatches.

Example: Memphis, TN → Sapulpa, OK (Atlanta → Denver freight)

A long-haul Atlanta → Denver load can run direct OTR or split through the Expedited Memphis–Sapulpa middle leg. The Expedited path adds ~90 miles, ~2 hours, and three T-Calls.

OTR Option (1,399 mi / 24 hrs)
Atlanta, GA → Denver, CO  (1,399 mi)
Expedited Option (1,489 mi / 26 hrs)
OTR Atlanta, GA → Memphis, TN (380 mi) EXP Memphis, TN → Atkins, AR (193 mi) T-Call EXP Atkins, AR → Sapulpa, OK (221 mi) T-Call OTR Sapulpa, OK → Denver, CO (693 mi) T-Call

Pattern: a freight move that OTR can complete in a single dispatch becomes four dispatches with three T-Calls when routed through Expedited’s middle leg — without any improvement in transit time for the customer.

Non-Self-Contained Operations — 80% Dependency on Non-EXP Trucks

The EXP Portion of the Orders Has 2.9% Deadhead
The Non-EXP Portion of the Orders Has 22.9% Deadhead
Jan Feb Mar Apr* YTD
Dispatched
on EXP		Loaded1,458,1641,535,4731,979,058576,6315,549,326
Empty43,23247,24056,30018,356165,128
Total1,501,3961,582,7132,035,358594,9875,714,454
Deadhead2.9%3.0%2.8%3.1%2.9%
Dispatched
Outside EXP		Loaded738,546796,8581,136,278341,0763,012,758
Empty217,107239,429337,28498,572892,392
Total955,6531,036,2871,473,562439,6483,905,150
Deadhead22.7%23.1%22.9%22.4%22.9%
TOTALLoaded2,196,7102,332,3313,115,336917,7078,562,084
Empty260,339286,669393,584116,9281,057,520
Total2,457,0492,619,0003,508,9201,034,6359,619,604
Deadhead10.6%10.9%11.2%11.3%11.0%

The roughly 8× deadhead gap (22.9% non-EXP vs. 2.9% EXP) is the most concrete cost signal of the non-self-contained model. Every order that is “assisted” by a non-EXP truck is paying that deadhead premium somewhere in the network.

Average Length of Haul (ALOH) of Non-EXP Dispatches
Average
313 mi
25th Percentile
168 mi
Median
273 mi
75th Percentile
417 mi

The non-EXP dispatch distribution is right-skewed and weighted toward 150–500 mile dispatches. These are not short “local” runs — the median is 273 miles, with the heaviest concentration in the 200–325-mile range and a long tail extending past 1,000 miles. The work being absorbed by non-EXP trucks looks much more like a true OTR dispatch than like a short pickup or delivery assist.

The Implication

Very little Expedited freight moves from L-Call to E-Call exclusively through EXP trucks. As Expedited grows (and OTR shrinks), Expedited is consuming more OTR capacity — but OTR is not being fairly compensated on the P&L for this assistance. Either (a) accounting needs to be changed so the cost of non-EXP assistance is allocated where it’s incurred, (b) a new pickup / delivery strategy needs to bring more freight back inside the EXP envelope, or (c) the freight mix itself needs to change so that what we run on Expedited actually fits the model.
