T3
Workspace, Environment & Work-From-Home Policy
Physical setup, ergonomics, leadership presence, and the team impact of the updated on-site policy
Physical Workspace & Ergonomics
  • July 13 was the first required on-site day following a remote-to-onsite transition. Approx. 1 hour per person required to reach full operational status — due to inconsistent workstation setup (monitors, docking stations, peripherals).
  • Team works 12-hour shifts requiring a 3-monitor setup to operate efficiently. Shared desks require reconfiguration each shift — compounding start-of-day delays.
  • Several chairs were broken or non-adjustable. Team members reported ergonomic discomfort including improper chair height and elbow pain. A request to source replacements from Dubuque surplus was submitted (Fred notified).
  • Seating arrangements were not yet optimized for role proximity or cross-functional collaboration. (Addressed post-walk — see Updated Seating Layout below.)
Updated Seating Layout — Post-Walk Reorganization

Following the on-site walk, the floor was reorganized to put related lanes and shifts in closer proximity. The updated layout below groups dayside teams by lane (color-coded), keeps the 4x4 nights cluster co-located, and positions the EXPE Office and Brian Ford's office adjacent to the active dispatch floor — making it materially easier for leadership to be physically present rather than messaging from a private office.

  • What changed: Lane teams are now seated together rather than scattered across the floor, which directly addresses the original observation that "seating arrangements were not yet optimized for role proximity or cross-functional collaboration."
  • What to watch: Whether shared-desk reconfiguration time at each shift start drops now that the same lane occupies the same physical block, and whether handoff quality improves now that incoming dispatchers sit at the same desk as their lane counterpart.
  • Open questions on the new layout: Are the visitor desks (purple block) sufficient for the typical visitor load, or do they need to grow? Does the 4x4 Nights cluster have the right adjacencies for night-shift cross-coverage?
On-Floor Leadership Presence
  • Floor presence was minimal and inconsistent during AM hours. Observed communication was delivered via electronic messaging from a private office for the majority of the day, with intermittent floor presence increasing slightly in the afternoon. Written messages and directives substituted for real-time floor coaching, and team members reported a perception of limited guidance, falling back on self-directed problem solving during the transition period.
  • Phone load was not distributed evenly. Brian was on the phone for the majority of the day. Regina was on and off. Tyler answered calls only when no one else was available — suggesting either a confidence gap or a behavioral pattern that needs to be surfaced and addressed.
  • Lane 147 specifically: Amye frequently directed what needed to happen rather than enabling the team to work through problems themselves. The team was told the answer rather than walked through the reasoning — a pattern that limits skill development and keeps the lane dependent on a single leader.
Work-From-Home Policy & 12-Hour Shift Impact

The updated WFH policy — requiring onsite work unless a formal accommodation exists — emerged as a recurring topic in floor conversations during the walk. The observations below cover how the policy is landing, the role-fairness questions it raises, what the team values about being together in person, the daily-routine reality of the 12-hour shift on top of the policy, and the hybrid structure the team proposed.

How the policy is landing
  • The updated policy — requiring onsite work unless a formal accommodation exists — has created challenges for some team members.
  • Employees understand the intent of the policy, but several noted that the shift places constraints on their personal routines and work-life balance.
  • A few individuals mentioned considering moving, since they currently rent, to reduce the burden of commuting — a direct retention signal worth tracking.
Role variability & fairness questions
  • The team pointed out that some employees are fully remote and are not required to come into the office. While team members recognize that roles differ based on business needs, this contrast naturally raises questions about consistency and fairness.
  • Several Expedited specialists currently sit in remote locations (Tucson, Danville KY, Springfield MO, Papillion NE, Hudson WI). The distinction between "remote by role" and "remote by accommodation" is not always clear to the team on the floor.
What the team values about being in-person
  • When the team is physically together, collaboration is noticeably stronger. Questions are answered more quickly, tribal knowledge is shared more naturally, and issues are resolved faster.
  • Team members frequently referenced how much easier it is to speak out loud to the group rather than rely on chat tools — verbal exchange keeps the group in sync without the latency and fragmentation of Teams / email. (Reinforces the collaboration patterns observed in T6.)
12-hour shift reality — the daily footprint

The 12-hour-shift schedule is what makes the WFH question different here than in a 9-to-5 environment. Team members shared their actual daily schedules — the total footprint from wake to post-shift wind-down runs roughly 16 hours for a 12-hour paid shift.

Time Window Activity
04:00 – 04:30Wake up
04:30 – 05:30Get ready
05:00 – 05:30Commute
05:30 – 06:00Arrive at work
… 12-hour shift …On-duty operational coverage
18:00 – 20:00Depart work
18:30 – 20:30Arrive home, eat, and prepare for the next day
  • Many team members noted that when working remotely, they can manage small personal tasks — laundry, meal prep, quick errands — that are difficult to handle when onsite for extended hours.
  • Despite the long days, several employees said they manage because their off-week allows them to participate in family activities and school events — the 7-on / 7-off rotation is the primary mechanism that makes the onsite 12-hour schedule sustainable for them. (See T12 for the full schedule and retention implications.)
  • The combination of ~16-hour daily footprint + 7 consecutive days + onsite-only policy is the fatigue pattern worth surfacing to leadership when evaluating the policy trade-off.
Proposed hybrid structure
  • A recurring suggestion from multiple team members was a hybrid model: Monday, Wednesday, and Friday onsite, with Tuesday and Thursday as flexible days.
  • Employees felt this structure could maintain operational alignment (with fixed anchor days for in-person collaboration, coaching, and handoff quality) while offering improved work-life balance.
  • The suggestion came from multiple independent conversations, not a single voice — indicating broad-based support for a middle path rather than a full reversal of the policy.
T4
Technology
EPM, AS400, 8x8, the spreadsheet, and the planning workflow that runs across all of them
EPM — Expedited Planning Module
Original intent vs. current reality
  • EPM was originally scoped to replace the shared Excel spreadsheet as the day-to-day operational system of record. Today, the spreadsheet is still the system of record and EPM is functioning primarily as a dispatch/load-management layer in front of AS400.
  • The team has been on EPM for ~11 months and the basic day-to-day management of operations still cannot be done inside the tool. Multiple team members confirmed they could not do their job in EPM as currently configured.
  • The single largest blocker is the one-way sync between EPM and AS400. A one-way sync structurally cannot serve as a system of record because data corrections cannot round-trip cleanly.
  • The Express function exists and is reportedly closer in shape to what the team actually needs (closer to the spreadsheet workflow). However, it was never formally rolled out — only Sarah and Krista use it regularly, while Brian Bratton and Josh were trained in Planning. There has been no formal "we are switching to this" communication.
  • A driver communication portal is reportedly in development to address some of the visibility gaps below, but no demo has yet been observed by Operations leadership.
Integration & data flow
  • One-way integration: EPM sends data to AS400, but AS400 does not return updates to EPM. All corrections must be executed in EPM to avoid data discrepancies — there is no rollback from the AS400 side.
  • Dummy appointment workaround: Loads cannot be planned in EPM unless delivery times exist in AS400. Team uses a "0022" dummy appointment to proceed, then must return later to update actual times — creating structured rework on every affected load.
  • T-call dispatch limitations: T-call dispatch requires both legs to exist in EPM. When a load has not been planned in EPM, AS400 is used instead — resulting in dual-system execution and increased error risk.
  • Auto-set logic dependency: EPM automatically sets Pre-T Calls (PTCs) and drops, but only when prerequisite data (PTCs, stop sequence) is fully configured. Loads planned before prerequisites are established cause misordered stops and downstream corrections.
  • Lane 147 multi-stop pathing: EPM pathing for multi-stop lanes (CA → TX) is non-linear, requiring manual reordering of PTC stops. The module appears optimized for point-to-point moves with a mid-swap — creating usability gaps for complex lanes.
  • Manual PTC entry time: When auto-set logic cannot be used, each Pre-T Call location, date, and time must be entered manually (~2–3 min per stop). Subsequent PTCs are calculated manually at +6 hr increments — repetitive and time-consuming on Lane 147.
  • No trailer data in EPM: EPM is positioned as the dispatch tool for Expedited, yet it does not contain trailer information. Planners must pivot to other systems to confirm trailer number, location, or status — breaking the "single pane of glass" promise and contributing to the wrong-trailer risk documented in T8 (Driver Management).
Bugs & UX friction
  • Sidebar collapse: Every entry into EPM requires manually collapsing the sidebar to view all planning lines. This is a required non-value-add click on every single module entry.
  • AM/PM entry error: A load was incorrectly planned at 17:00 instead of 05:00. The error blocked correct dispatch — resolution required waiting until the driver was removed from the load before the EPM entry could be deleted and replanned.
  • Unplan destroys T-calls: Unplanning a shuffled load in EPM inadvertently deletes associated T-calls and planned legs. Re-planning then triggers an error: "load not available." No warning is presented before the destructive action.
  • Search bug: Search function requires users to delete and slowly re-enter numbers to avoid returning all loads instead of the searched item.
  • Row zeroing bug: Randomly, the bottom row of the scheduling interface zeros out or switches to the wrong day — causing hard-to-diagnose planning errors.
  • Backward-planning bug: Planning backward when "zeros" are present on the second row causes an error; planning forward corrects it.
  • Layout inefficiency: The planning button bar occupies excessive screen space, forcing excessive scrolling. Search bar is not auto-focused on open. Concerns raised about blank space and layout density.
  • Visibility window: Team needs visibility to the prior 36 hours and forward 36 hours to match driver timelines. Current view is insufficient.
  • "Express" function: Mixed guidance on whether to use it — some told not to use it, no documented adoption decision, no SOPs.
  • Truck number field: Auto-populates from previous leg — must be manually deleted when planning a new leg, or errors result.
T-Call cascade — unintended removal across divisions

A specific EPM behavior with operational consequences that reach beyond Expedited: when a leg of a trip is removed or a load is X’d out in EPM, the system automatically removes all associated T-Calls — including OTR T-Calls. The cascade fires regardless of why the leg or load was removed, which means the same destructive action is triggered whether the planner is correcting an error, temporarily un-planning to re-plan, or making a permanent change.

  • Trigger: any leg removal or load X-out in EPM, regardless of intent.
  • Cascade: EPM auto-removes all T-Calls associated with that leg/load. The cascade is not scoped to Expedited — OTR T-Calls are removed alongside Expedited T-Calls, which means an Expedited planner’s correction can silently clear handoff expectations on the OTR side without OTR planners being notified.
  • No distinction between "temporary" and "permanent" removal: the system treats a 30-second un-plan-to-replan the same as a deliberate permanent removal. There is no soft-delete, no "hold T-Calls for replanning" option, and no confirmation dialog warning the planner about what is about to be cleared.
  • Operational risks created:
    1. Missed handoffs — when a transfer point is silently cleared, the receiving driver / next leg has no expectation set.
    2. Incorrect routing — once the drop expectation is gone from the system, there is nothing routing the driver toward the intended swap point.
    3. Driver continuing on a load without an intended drop — in the worst case, the driver continues past where the handoff was supposed to occur because the drop has been removed from their plan.
  • What would resolve it (illustrative, not prescriptive): a confirmation dialog that explicitly lists what T-Calls (Expedited and OTR) will be cleared by the action; a soft-delete / "hold T-Calls for replanning" mode that preserves handoff expectations during a known-temporary edit; a notification to the OTR planner whose T-Call is being cleared by an Expedited-side action.
Ideas that came out of observation
  • Fishbowl concept: Customer service could flag loads as "EXP" (expedited) in EPM, auto-populating a list from which planners drag and drop loads onto trucks — eliminating manual search and reducing missed freight.
  • Auto-detect workday: Plan to auto-detect workdays when drivers check in at start, auto-fill pay without manual entry. Missed check-in = load flagged as missed scheduled shift. Reduces PTO/time-off exception volume.
  • Geofence-triggered plans: Suggestion for system to automatically execute plans based on geofence arrival rather than manual dispatch triggers.
  • Driver visibility to swap partner: Drivers need to see swap partner info and ETAs after start-of-day; partner distance should populate after arrival context.
  • Automatic load notifications: Centralized EPM notification 2 hours prior to start time with load and truck details; auto-update handling on changes.
AS400 — Legacy Operational System
  • AS400 remains essential: Still required for partial order updates with run times, T-calls when EPM lacks the button, and certain dispatch configurations. Not replaceable by EPM today.
  • Session timeout issue: AS400 times out and logs users out even on auto-refreshing screens. Some users have a significantly longer timeout than others — suggesting a per-user configuration setting that is not standardized across the team. This did not occur on the previous JCT system. No ticket submitted as of observation.
  • Click-through configuration inconsistency: Jordan was missing the standard behavior where double-clicking a load number auto-pops the AS400 detail screen. A ticket was submitted during the walk and the fix was a simple configuration toggle — turned on for him, scheduled to be tested that night. This implies AS400 is not configured the same for the entire team, and that other team members may be missing functionality without knowing it.
8x8 — Driver Communication Platform
  • Speed comparison observed directly:
    Outbound message via 8x8: 3 seconds  |  Via AS400: ~1 minute
    Message retrieval via 8x8: 13 seconds  |  Via AS400: 2 min 13 sec
    8x8 is approximately 20× faster for driver communication. Drivers also note that 8x8 messages are easier to read because typing on the driver app produces fewer spelling errors.
  • Single-queue limitation: A user can only be logged into one 8x8 queue at a time. This is the core reason 8x8 hasn't been expanded — broader rollout would require a team member to see every lane's messages, or to log out and back in when going on break. Need to confirm with the vendor whether a multi-queue or sub-user configuration exists.
  • Limited to Lane 147 and accessed through a single shared login (Amye's). Broader use without message grouping causes message flooding. Single login creates a dependency risk.
  • Alan's 8x8 desktop app is broken — has been using a web browser workaround for years. The web workaround does not show driver phone numbers in the contact card, which is what creates the "generic number" inbound experience described below. Ticket submitted, directed to Carter Lee for investigation.
  • Inbound driver calls — generic number behavior: Phone does not audibly ring — calls can be missed without active headset use. Twenty to twenty-three calls were missed across two separate observation days, requiring a callback. Inbound calls display a generic number when the driver is not yet stored in 8x8 — once the dispatcher updates that contact entry, the driver's name persists for future calls. Both the web app and the mobile app must be open simultaneously for the lookup to populate.
  • Driver communications must run through 8x8, not personal cell phones. Some employees have been calling drivers from cell phones, which means there is no auditable record of the conversation. This has already created at least one wrongful-termination dispute that could not be resolved because the conversation could not be retrieved. 8x8 captures the conversation by design — that is one of the main reasons to standardize on it.
  • Leadership visibility into 8x8 messages: Today, a leader cannot read another user's 8x8 messages without that user's password. A ticket was previously raised requesting sub-user / shared visibility configuration; vendor came back saying it could not be done. This needs a second pass — leadership oversight of driver communications is a basic operating requirement.
  • Utilization reporting: No visibility today into who is logged into 8x8 vs. who is sitting at their desk not logged in. On at least one weekend, two of the three scheduled team members were not logged into the queue, leaving a single person to handle all calls. Reporting access has been requested but not yet granted.
The Spreadsheet — De-Facto System of Record
  • The team primarily uses a SharePoint Excel spreadsheet for daily operations management. Data is manually pulled from AS400 and entered into the spreadsheet each shift.
  • The spreadsheet is treated as the system of record ("the Bible") — it highlights duplicates, tracks loads, drivers, breaks, and status. EPM is the dispatch tool, but the spreadsheet governs the day.
  • Manual copy time measured per lane:
    Lane 147 (CA/TX): ~4.5 hours/day  |  Lane 155 (OK/TN): ~2.0 hours/day  |  Lane 148 (IN/NC): ~2.5 hours/day
  • Color-coding/symbols in 147 spreadsheet: Blue font = planned in EPM; Bold = needs truck assignment; Peach shading = driver assigned; Gray strikethrough = leg complete. This system is understood by the day shift but not standardized or documented.
  • Planners aim to stay half a day ahead — planning the afternoon in the morning and next morning in the evening. Due to system complexity and frequent changes, planning rarely extends further to avoid wasted rework.
  • Team is consistently in "firefighting mode" — reacting to immediate issues rather than planning 2–4 days ahead. This causes missed freight opportunities when competitors have already claimed available loads. OTR planners often find loads scheduled 2–3 days in advance; expedited needs can change within hours.
  • Brian uses self-built macros to automate daily truck checks, driver mile reviews, and templated messaging — significantly reducing manual work. Not standardized across the team. The same macros could be built in 8x8, but adoption is inconsistent — every dispatcher uses them differently.
  • Inconsistent driver layout in spreadsheet: The driver-by-driver layout depends entirely on who the dispatcher is. Some lanes are well-organized and clearly notated; others are partially completed and require interpretation by whoever is reading them. This makes shift handoff and cross-coverage harder than it needs to be.
Planning Workflow & Freight Sourcing
How freight gets sourced today
  • Team searches for available loads via the internal "AOS work with planning" screen — looking for expedited loads not already claimed by OTR teams — rather than external load boards.
  • External load sourcing not used: When asked whether the team taps into external load boards or other arms of the company's freight tools, the consistent answer was no. The team's freight search is bounded by what is already in the internal system. This is a training and tooling gap, not a willingness gap.
  • An automation is reportedly being developed by John to auto-tag and guarantee specific loads for the Expedited team, preventing OTR from claiming them. Confirmed during the walk that this is technically achievable today via Freight Science (auto-populate EXP and OF on inbound loads for established lanes); it does not stop OTR planners from re-claiming a load after the fact, but it solves the front-end intake. Northern lanes already have this set up; newer lanes (e.g., Tennessee → Oklahoma) still need to be configured.
  • OTR re-claiming observed: Multiple instances were observed where Expedited had assigned a load 3–4 days out and OTR came back in and changed it back to OTR — leaving the Expedited team to find a replacement load (and the original driver already assigned) on short notice.
Planning is coordination, not just finding freight

A consistent floor observation that is easy to miss in the metrics: the planning role on Expedited is not primarily about locating loads. The bigger time and risk sink is the monitoring and coordination work that happens once a load is selected. Every load on most lanes requires the planner to actively choreograph multiple moving pieces, none of which they directly control.

  • OTR first-mile coordination: Because ~94% of orders on key cost centers depend on a non-EXP truck for pickup or delivery, the planner has to confirm an OTR driver is dispatched and on the way to the first mile before the Expedited shuttle plan can be locked in. If the OTR driver is late, every downstream leg slips.
  • Delivery monitoring for the shuttle: The planner is also actively monitoring the OTR delivery side to make sure the load arrives on time at the swap point, so the receiving Expedited driver doesn't sit. This is real-time tracking work, not planning work.
  • Mid-stream rework when the front- or back-end fails: When the OTR driver runs late, has a service issue, or T-calls onto a different truck, the planner has to rebuild the shuttle plan in real time — frequently while still answering driver calls and updating the spreadsheet.
  • Implication: Even a 100% perfect freight-selection process would not materially reduce the planner's workload, because the workload is dominated by coordinating the upstream and downstream OTR legs. Finding the load is maybe 20% of the job; making sure it actually executes end-to-end is the other 80%.
T5
HOS, Split Shifts & Disruption Recovery
Policy gaps, GeoTab configuration, and unplanned event response
What Was Observed
  • Multiple HOS disruption scenarios observed due to a local festival causing 3+ hour traffic delays, burning 2–4 on-duty hours and forcing dispatch to reassign loads to maintain compliance and meet delivery commitments.
  • Adverse Conditions exception observed: In the festival incident above, the driver was granted two additional driving hours under the Adverse Conditions exception rather than running a split shift. The team had to be reminded what this exception is called and when it applies.
  • "Adverse Conditions" definition is not clearly documented for this team. Most assumed it applied only to weather (blizzards, severe storms). It can also apply to major accidents and unforeseen non-weather events that prevent forward progress, but use requires a call-in for approval. Documentation needed so the team knows when and how to invoke it.
  • Team expressed a desire to use the split-shift option in these disruption scenarios but stated it was "not allowed" for Expedited operations.
  • Post-observation follow-up with Chad Smith: Chad was unaware this belief existed on the floor. The expectation is for drivers to return home for a full 10-hour break after each shift when on schedule — making split shifts unnecessary under normal conditions. Chad did not confirm a formal prohibition and the split-shift option has been put back on the table for use in disruption scenarios.
  • GeoTab rule set: Split breaks are currently disallowed for Expedited in GeoTab. When drivers log "sleeper berth," the system converts it to "off duty," preventing a valid split break and potentially causing violations. The workaround is manual log editing by drivers.
  • Driver coaching gap: Even with split breaks back on the table, drivers need to know exactly how to log them in GeoTab. The current concern is that drivers log split breaks incorrectly, which is part of why the option was discouraged in the first place. A short, documented training (with screenshots) is needed before re-enabling the option in practice.
  • Disruption types observed: festival traffic, weather delays, swap-location maintenance delays, tire failures. Each can consume 2–4 on-duty hours.
T6
Communication Overload & Interruptions
Volume, fragmentation, and the cost of context-switching
Interruption Counts — Observed July 13
Time BlockCalls InChat / MsgWalk-UpsNotes
0700–1200117372,000+ emails accumulated from 7 days off. Walk-ups averaged 7–10 min each.
1300–16004611150+ Teams messages within 4 hours. Walk-up average maintained at 7–10 min.
What Was Observed — Communication Patterns
  • The team uses six or more platforms simultaneously: Email, Phone (8x8), Microsoft Teams, AS400 messaging, Excel spreadsheet, GeoTab, Trailer Suite, and EPM — all during a single shift.
  • No A/B shift boundaries or channel rules in Teams. Assumption is that everyone reads everything. No message tagging or routing by shift group. CSRs (also on rotating 24-hour coverage) implemented a similar pattern with IT — that solution can likely be reused here.
  • 25 IT-created Teams chats exist for Expedited alone, plus an unknown number of one-off chats started by individuals. IT can only pull a list of the IT-created chats; the one-offs are invisible to reporting.
  • Pop-up notifications fire roughly every 2 minutes on a typical day — every Teams message, every inbox arrival, every chat creates a banner that pulls focus. Several team members did not know how to turn this off until shown during the walk.
  • Email on return from a 7-day off rotation: 2,000+ messages requiring triage before productive work can begin. No email filtering, archiving, or priority tagging standard in place.
  • Teams "@mention" routing is not being used as a way to keep individual users from drowning. Targeted use of @mentions could let everyone see a message while only notifying the specific person being asked. This needs to be tested against the current Teams configuration.
  • Communication style from leadership characterized as "too many emails with changing priorities" — causing confusion and a lack of clear direction. Team noted shifting focus creates competing definitions of success. A stated example: priority swings between PMs, Litix, and load movement without a documented escalation rule for how to choose when they conflict.
  • Regina relies heavily on inefficient text-message communication for driver management — outside formal tracked channels. For minor updates this is acceptable; for material changes (e.g., re-routing, pay changes), a phone call on 8x8 is the right channel and is currently being skipped.
  • Cecil weekend example: Multiple distractions appeared to delay action on a single open item over the weekend; a 15-minute phone call resolved what had been pending for hours. This pattern (fragmented chat → no resolution → phone call closes it) repeated several times during the walk.
  • One person handling all tasks simultaneously was observed to be more productive than multiple people dividing attention across platforms — a signal of context-switching cost.
  • Centralized SharePoint site established: A single SharePoint site has been created during the walk to act as the centralized reference location for SOPs, quick-reference materials, and shift-specific updates. Operations leadership will be added as admins. Goal is one place to look — instead of 25+ chats, 2,000+ emails, and tribal memory.
  • Shift-level priority messaging concept (Mo / Ashley group): Mo described a platform used by Ashley's group where a single message can be tagged for a specific shift, surfaced as the "top of day" item, and categorized by week. A demo has been requested. If it works as described, this is a much better fit for Expedited's shift structure than today's broadcast-everything Teams model.
T7
Shift Handoff & Accountability
Handoff quality, overnight ownership, and workload imbalance
What Was Observed
  • No formal brief or debrief structure was observed across any shift transition. All handoffs were informal verbal exchanges — no documented format, no required fields, no confirmation of understanding.
  • Returning from a 7-day off rotation, dispatchers cannot easily catch up on what happened in their absence. The morning brief covers the prior day or weekend, not the prior week. Major personnel changes can land before the brief and are not always announced — one specific shift change happened on a Friday afternoon and was still not formally announced in Monday's morning brief, even though the affected team members had already heard about it informally.
  • Night shift regularly fails to complete planned load updates or spreadsheet maintenance. Day shift arrives to unresolved backlogs — Mondays are the worst, with 33–40+ open items routinely observed after the off-rotation weekend.
  • No accountability mechanism exists for overnight spreadsheet maintenance. The problem is known and recurring — team members are aware but no ownership has been assigned.
  • Day shift handles all planning and setup, creating a perceived workload asymmetry with night shift. This imbalance is a morale concern. However, the imbalance is not uniform between people — Davey routinely plans on nights without issue; some night-shift counterparts do not. This is a coaching/training pattern, not a structural one.
  • Driver communication is handled inconsistently between shifts, resulting in a non-uniform experience for drivers and gaps in continuity. Drivers will specifically ask for a certain dispatcher because they prefer that person's communication style.
  • No consistent weekend handoff standard. Accountability for weekend coverage is unclear. On the off-week, chat documentation drops off significantly, and on at least one off-week the prior shift left no documented chat trail at all.
  • Driver-level history is not tracked between shifts. If a driver has an issue this week, the dispatcher next week has no easy way to see what happened. This makes coaching inconsistent — a driver may get a stern message from one shift and a soft one from another, on the same recurring issue. The team's accountability discipline depends on driver memory more than driver records.
  • Frank fits the same pattern as the multi-shift driver bucket above. Mostly driver-side, but instances were observed where a freight cutoff applied one week did not transfer to the next week's dispatcher, and the lane was run with stale rules.
  • Specific handoff failure observed: A load was abandoned in Las Cruces, NM and surfaced only in the next week's service meeting. There were no POAs, no chats, and no email trail explaining how it got there or what should happen next. The next-week dispatcher had nothing to act on.
  • Arkansas City example: 27 trailers were sitting at the Walmart yard. Night shift requested permission to bobtail in to retrieve some of them; the CSR rejected the request because the policy is to always bring a trailer in. The trailers stayed where they were; the issue went back and forth without a clear decision-maker. This is a partnership boundary that needs to be redrawn — CSR should not unilaterally veto an Expedited operational decision when the trailer pool is sitting idle.
Real Handoff Examples — How They Vary in the Wild

Shift-to-shift and week-to-week handoffs are handled inconsistently — not just within a single handoff, but across handoffs. Different people use different formats, different mediums (chat vs. email), and different conventions for flagging what is urgent. The receiving shift can’t develop a quick-scan habit because every handoff is laid out differently. The two real examples below illustrate the spread.

Example 1 — Saturday-evening chat note, Lane 147

Freeform prose, written into the lane chat, addressed to "team." No structure, no priority indicator.

Example 2 — Friday-evening email, Lanes 151 / 156

A different author, a different medium (email), a different format — structured driver-by-driver and truck-by-truck, with yellow highlights flagging what the writer felt was most important.

What the two examples show together
The two handoffs are by different people, on different days, in different mediums, with different structures, and with different conventions for marking urgency. Neither is wrong on its own — both contain the right information. But the variance between them is itself the problem. The next shift can’t build a habit of where to look first when every handoff is laid out differently. A consistent template — even something as simple as Open Items / Watch List / Driver Flags / FYI, applied regardless of medium or author — would let the receiving shift triage in seconds rather than minutes, and would surface the items that genuinely need follow-up instead of leaving them buried, highlighted inconsistently, or split across two emails.
Observed Exception Events
EventTriggerHow ResolvedRecurrence Risk
Wrong trailer attached — load destined FL, driver heading IN. Seal matched; destination not verified.Driver did not confirm destination on bills during loading callCaught post-scale; intervention requiredHigh — no standard loading call verification checklist confirmed
Driver shift delayed 4+ hours (11 AM departure)Unspecified disruption$100 extra pay offered as judgment call to prevent service failureMedium — no defined threshold for when/how extra pay is authorized
Driver offered load to Tucson; refused citing personal dissatisfaction and objections to travel arrangementsDriver morale / assignment frictionLoad not assigned; flexible delivery date allowed delayMedium — driver relationship issues accumulate and drive turnover
OTR truck defaulted on a covered expedited loadLast-minute OTR failureSignificant load rearrangement; driver Russell offered $100 extra payMedium — cross-division dependency on OTR creates recurring fragility
T8
Driver Management, Pay & Productivity
Pay structure, fairness concerns, communication gaps, and loading call compliance
Pay Structure — Observed
MarketDaily RateProgressionNotes
Texas / Arizona~$260/day+$5/wk at 90, 180, 365 daysNo separate safety, holiday, detention, or layover pay. Wage progression is the only incentive structure.
California~$280/daySame
  • Drivers are paid when available for dispatch but not run. Not paid if they call in unavailable when work was available.
  • If a truck is down, drivers may be assigned alternate work (yard checks) or placed in another truck.
  • No defect over-reporting concern observed — reporting appears appropriate and safety-aligned.
  • Pay discrepancy incident: One driver nearly resigned over receiving $280/day instead of the promised $300/day, plus being asked to drive an extra 30 miles daily. Issue resolved by honoring the agreed rate. Illustrates how small trust breaks drive turnover.
  • Shag driver productivity gap: One local shag driver consistently completes only one load/day while a peer in the same role completes two — both paid the same daily rate. Other team members confirmed the pattern, noting the driver "always finds a way to get out of doing X, Y, and Z." No accountability mechanism in place to address.
  • No individual-level KPIs exist for drivers or planners. Without published expectations, the team cannot coach to a standard, and chronic underperformance cannot be formally addressed (e.g., the shag driver case above, or repeated mistakes from a specific dispatcher). The "tattle-tell" gap noted on the floor is a downstream symptom of this — issues stay at the local level instead of bubbling up where ownership exists.
  • Audit needed: Variances found in paychecks related to detention pay awards — scope unknown. X-Pay, Detention, and Layover payment frequency to Expedited drivers should be audited.
Driver Communication & Loading Call Compliance
  • Driver schedule notifications are a manual step performed outside core workflow in AS400 or EPM. When missed or delayed, drivers report frustration and uncertainty about shift timing.
  • A driver's ability to make a loading call was disabled by the system completing the call rather than the driver — preventing the driver from executing a required step.
  • Auto-swap account workaround: In some cases, dispatchers have been logging into the driver's account to hit "start my day" or complete the loaded/empty call when the driver's app fails to do so. Per the team, this is the auto-swap account feature working as designed for that scenario; however, it is not consistently understood across the team and is not documented as an SOP.
  • Some drivers are delaying loading calls until they reach a swap point — suggesting pre-trip inspections may be bypassed at origin. No enforcement mechanism observed.
  • Pre-trip ordering issue: When asked, several drivers said they sometimes complete the pre-trip before hooking to the trailer. A thorough pre-trip cannot be completed before hooking — the lights cannot be tested without the trailer connected. This means a portion of pre-trip inspections are not being done in full. Reinforcement is needed at orientation and at swap-point coaching.
  • Wrong trailer incident: Driver attached to the wrong trailer — load was Florida-bound; driver was heading to Indiana. Seal number matched, destination was not verified during the loading call. Discovered post-scale. Trailer team is working on a separate trailer-side improvement that should reduce recurrence.
  • Pigtail line / lights issue: Drivers reported intermittent lights on hook-up that look like a trailer defect but are actually a loose pigtail connection. A small wedge ($7 on Amazon) is widely used by OTR drivers to bridge the gap and force the lights to engage. After being shown to a few Expedited drivers during the walk, follow-up confirmed the wedge solved the problem on the trucks where it was tried. Worth adding to standard small-parts kit and orientation training.
  • Basic equipment troubleshooting at orientation: Drivers in this fleet should know how to change a light bulb and perform basic equipment troubleshooting before they leave orientation. This is a fit with the "Elite Fleet" positioning and would meaningfully reduce small breakdown calls. Currently not part of the orientation curriculum.
  • During orientation, the fuel parameters communicated may not match current approved specifications. Request to update orientation materials to reflect accurate, current parameters.
  • Orientation depth gap: Current driver orientation was observed as a read-through of information rather than an interactive conversation. Presenters roll down a list of items without confirming comprehension. Orientation should include more back-and-forth interaction to make sure drivers actually understand what is being communicated — not just that it was delivered.
  • Driver milestone tracking is not consistent. When a driver mentions a child's graduation, an anniversary, or a major life event during orientation, those moments are not being captured anywhere. A central calendar (Outlook for some, spreadsheet for others, nothing for many) is the inconsistent norm. Following up on a driver's milestone is a high-impact, low-cost retention move that the team is not currently equipped to do at scale.
Driver Availability & Last-Minute Call-Ins
  • Driver call-ins and no-shows are a large, recurring operational concern. A meaningful share of drivers call in unavailable or no-show on assigned shifts, and the volume is high enough that planners build their day around it. Plans have to be rebuilt in real time, trucks sit idle while replacements are sourced, and on-time commitments are put at risk.
  • The impact is not evenly distributed — certain lanes absorb the fill-in work more often than others, which adds an unplanned load on the already-stretched planners covering those lanes.
  • No standing process was observed for backfill sourcing, prioritization, or documentation of the root cause of each call-in — making it difficult to distinguish between isolated incidents and patterns that should be addressed at the driver level.
  • Proposed lever — small attendance incentive: A modest weekly incentive (e.g., +$5/week, paid only for weeks with no unpaid attendance issues) was raised as a low-cost way to address the no-show / call-in pattern directly. It does not change the schedule or pay structure; it adds a small reward for the behavior we want to see. Pilot-able on a single lane to test the impact before broader rollout.
Driver Pay & Scheduling — Management-Time Sink

A finding that surfaced when totaling up where leadership time is going: driver pay and driver scheduling are almost entirely manual processes today, and they consume roughly a quarter of management's time. This is time that is not going into coaching, customer issues, or strategic work — it is going into spreadsheet entry and reconciliation.

  • Driver scheduling is performed outside core workflow. After orientation, a driver's onto-the-lane assignment is done by hand. Day-to-day shift scheduling, time-off coverage, and lane reassignments are tracked across Outlook, the shared Excel spreadsheet, and individual leader notes — there is no single source of truth.
  • The 90/180/365 progression is on a manual spreadsheet that flags driver hit dates. When a hit date arrives, a leader still has to manually open the driver record, update the lane assignment, and confirm the pay change. The spreadsheet reminds; it doesn't act.
  • Pay variance reconciliation is also manual. Variances on detention, X-Pay, and layover surface paycheck-by-paycheck rather than through an automated audit. Each finding requires manual investigation against the source records.
  • Estimated leadership time impact: roughly 25%. Across the leadership team, approximately a quarter of the working week is going into supporting driver pay and scheduling administration. That is a structural overhead — not a process owned by a coordinator or automation, but an unallocated cost the leaders are absorbing personally.
  • Implication: If even half of this overhead were automated or assigned to a dedicated coordinator role, leadership capacity would expand meaningfully. This is one of the highest-leverage investments available — both for leadership burnout (T12) and for capacity to actually drive Expedited improvements.
Elite Fleet — Words Don't Match the Hiring Standard

A direct call-out worth elevating from the orientation observations: we want this to be the Elite Fleet of drivers, but our hiring standard does not reflect that ambition. The gap is real, and it shows up in driver behavior on the floor.

  • The orientation script positions Expedited drivers as "intentionally selected" and as "upholding a standard at the highest level." This is the Blue Angel Driver Standard from the strategy slide — top-tier professionalism, urgency, precision.
  • The actual hiring criteria are baseline. There is no formal selection screen, no specialized assessment, no "Elite Fleet" qualifier in recruiting. A driver who meets standard onboarding requirements goes onto Expedited the same way they would go onto OTR.
  • Behavior on the floor reflects the gap. Pre-trip shortcuts, loaded-call delays, no-shows, refusal of assignments based on personal preference — these are not Elite Fleet behaviors, and they show up frequently enough that the team treats them as the norm rather than the exception.
  • Two paths forward, both legitimate:
    1. Earn the language: Elevate the hiring criteria — formal selection process, assessment, references — so the orientation script is true. Expedited becomes a role drivers aspire to move into from OTR.
    2. Soften the language: Bring orientation in line with current selection so we are not making a promise to drivers we are not delivering on. Less aspirational, but honest.
  • Connection to the autonomous strategy: If Expedited is the path to autonomous, the driver profile during the crawl phase actually matters more, not less. These drivers are setting the standard a future autonomous fleet will need to operate against — that is a meaningful argument for raising the bar, not lowering it.
T9
Freight Finding & Network Coverage
Volume gaps, internal competition, borrowed freight, and the coverage problem
What Was Observed
  • Finding freight is consistently identified by all team members as the hardest part of the role and the largest area of weakness. A dedicated planner was suggested to help manage this workload — Customer Service stepping in is not what the team is asking for.
  • Re-frame: The harder part is not selecting freight — the team is good at finding loads. The harder part is executing the freight we select. Freight that is geographically reachable (e.g., Area 5) often has no truck capacity, and freight where we have capacity (e.g., Area 7 / Walmart) is behind on shipping. So the gap is between where freight is and where we have capacity to take it — not the search itself.
  • When lanes lack sufficient volume, freight is pulled from other lanes or regions (Nebraska, Kansas, Colorado) to build full loads — introducing inefficiency and shifting risk rather than solving the underlying demand gap.
  • Lane 155 example: the Sapulpa → Memphis lane regularly grabs Atlanta → Denver freight, splitting it into four dispatches with three T-Calls when OTR could have moved it as a single dispatch. The full leg-by-leg comparison is in T2 — Out-of-Route Miles; the take-away here is that this is the pattern, not a one-off.
  • Tennessee → Oklahoma lane grew too fast: The lane is established but freight has not caught up. The team is still trying to find loads on it weekly, with no formal "OF = EXP" tagging set up in Freight Science yet. Northern lanes already have that tagging in place; new lanes are being missed.
  • Internal competition: Parallel lanes (e.g., Kansas City to East Coast) route freight away from Expedited lanes. Texas freight going east is often routed through KC, bypassing Expedited coverage. Five people on the bidding team can reject lanes based on rates without clear communication — and the company appears to be bidding on lanes in areas it does not service.
  • We have bid against ourselves. On at least one lane, Expedited bid against Solutions and lost. This needs to be visible to whoever is sequencing bids so it doesn't repeat.
  • Walmart Arkansas City disruption: Closes every Monday, creating shortages Mondays and Tuesdays. Recently closed without notice for an inventory event (scanning failure) for a full week — stranding Expedited coverage. Shag drivers assigned filler loads on weekends just to stay productive.
  • Lanes without consistent connected loads cause missed handoffs and scrambling to avoid empty miles — leading to suboptimal routings (e.g., Texas → Wisconsin instead of 400 empty miles).
  • Route line creation after dispatch leads to pay and stop calculation mismatches — causing financial errors and poor operational visibility. This is a company-wide issue, not Expedited-specific. EPM is creating more route issues, not fewer; the why behind several of those is still being worked. References Andrew Slat's prior work saving millions through automation and reporting.
Network Design vs. Capacity — Where the Deadhead Is Coming From

A key finding during the walk was that several of the lanes flagged as inefficient are not just freight problems — they are network design problems. The Expedited points are not co-located with where we land trucks today, which means we are baking in deadhead before the first load is touched.

  • Memphis: Average deadhead to the lane is ~220 miles. Trucks are bouncing from the Goodlettsville / Nashville area to Memphis to grab loads because we don't land enough trucks in Memphis directly. T-calling Memphis was already a known bad fit for this same reason — not enough trucks land there.
  • Cocoa, FL: Average deadhead ~224 miles. Same pattern — network landing pattern doesn't match the Expedited point.
  • Liberal, KS: A capacity-rate-supportable lane on paper, but we don't land trucks anywhere near Liberal. We land in Colorado, Texas, or Oklahoma, and have to bring a truck across to grab the load. The rate supports it, but the network design forces the deadhead.
  • Dakota City → Norcross: Capacity-driven shortfall. The market is ~70 overbooked daily, so trucks are being pulled from far away just to cover the load. The cost driver here is capacity, not network shape.
  • Amarillo: Pure network design — we don't land trucks in Amarillo, period. Every load there starts with deadhead from another market.
  • Edgerton example walked through: Loaded miles matched the analysis on every leg; the variance was an extra 220 empty miles to reposition from Goodlettsville to Memphis to pick up the next load. That single repositioning is what pushed the lane over.
  • Joint statement to use externally: Our network design and our market imbalances, where our Expedited points are located, are causing increased deadhead and ultimately increased cost. This is not a single-cause problem — it's capacity, market balance, and network design all stacking on the same lanes.
  • The right framing for lane decisions: as one company, the right comparison is not "is Expedited the most efficient way to run this lane" — it is "is the lane more efficient under Expedited or under OTR?" If OTR is more efficient, the load should run under OTR even if Expedited would still book it. Worked examples and per-lane financial impact live in T2 — Out-of-Route Miles, Strategic Cost Signal; aggregate magnitude is captured in the same section.
Service Failure Correlation — Multiple Curves Pointing the Same Way

A separate analysis (developed independently by Carter and corroborated during the walk) shows several operational curves trending together starting in late 2025. The pattern aligns with what was observed on the floor. The two charts below capture the magnitude of the shift.

  • Service failures took an initial drop coinciding with the 1IBI cutover (expected — system integration). Service then started recovering as integrations stabilized.
  • A second decline began in November and has continued through the most recent data. The morning-call service reporting was paused during this period, which is one possible explanation, but the operational metrics independently support the trend.
  • Number of planning instances per week is up.
  • Expedited seated truck count has climbed since December — and the more seats added, the more the downward service curve has accelerated.
  • Dispatches per truck have climbed materially.
  • T-calls have climbed.
  • The pattern reads as: the more we have leaned into Expedited (more seats, more dispatches, more T-calls), the more service has degraded. This is consistent with the floor observation that we are "horrible at picking up T-call loads" — the recovery muscle isn't there to absorb the volume.
  • Hessler's broader framing (corroborated): load count has not grown materially in four years. We are not adding to the top of the funnel; we are reallocating volume from OTR into Expedited. That creates disgruntled OTR (fewer miles), under-fed teams (their freight got moved), and an Expedited operation that can't catch up.
  • Hessler note: for the first time in 7+ years, the Northeast and Southeast are overbooked — meaning the network is running tighter than it ever has. Some of that capacity is going into back-haul freight that historically ran empty into the Midwest, which is a positive shift — but it is reshaping the market in ways the lane plans assumed wouldn't happen.
Where Expedited Speed Wins — Lane-Level Signal

A handful of lane-level pricing/speed comparisons came up that are worth capturing. These are the lanes where the Expedited model has a clear, defensible value to the customer.

  • Texas → Calgary: Currently running ~36 hours. A team configuration could cut that by approximately a quarter, giving a meaningful speed advantage at a defensible rate.
  • Wytheville, VA → Indianapolis and Wytheville → Kansas: Solo rate is workable. The pitch becomes "team-level transit at solo-team economics" — faster than a single OTR driver, more predictable than a spot booking.
  • The pattern: lanes where the customer values speed and the network already lands trucks on at least one end are the natural fit for Expedited's value story. Lanes that fail either test (no time-sensitivity, or no truck landing) should be evaluated against the OTR baseline.
T10
Equipment & Trailer Operations
Trailer pool, tire issues, PM scheduling, and bobtail constraints
What Was Observed
  • Trailer location and availability: Team spends 30 min–1 hour per incident locating and verifying a trailer is empty. On-screen trailer tools are often inaccurate, requiring manual oversight. The trailer team is actively working to improve this and progress is expected.
  • Dedicated trailer pool not feasible under current operations: CA (Colton) has live loads across multiple business lines (OTR, Solutions); TX pickups are dispersed with many touchpoints; Colton experiencing 3–4 days of shortages; bobtailing into Colton is disallowed, causing driver waits or repositioning for empties. Worth keeping on the radar but not actionable today.
  • Empty bobtail dispatch placeholder workaround: Bobtail dispatches require a placeholder ("fake") trailer number processed through an empty call upon arrival, with the placeholder cleared from the driver profile afterward. Flagged as a risk: this workaround is going to cause data and accounting issues over time and should be replaced with a proper bobtail dispatch path in the system.
  • Trailer H40323 tire incident: Three tire replacements by Love's — immediately after one newly replaced tire "popped off the bead." Photos provided to maintenance for warranty recovery. Maintenance building a report to detect whether this is a trend on same-position replacements within 7 days. Vendor warranty recovery on this incident only happened because the right relationship was in place at the moment — a documented escalation path would make this repeatable.
  • No standard mechanism to escalate vendor maintenance issues for warranty recovery. The H40323 case is a concrete example of how a single Expedited incident can grow into vendor money back if it is documented and routed correctly. Today this happens by exception, not by standard.
  • PM scheduling: Managed by Amye; visibility to others is limited and often not far enough in advance, causing driver rescheduling disruptions. Unclear to the team who needs the visibility, when, and where the priority sits. Need to improve communication cadence so driver leaders see items earlier.
  • Quartzsite tire bank: Has been in progress for months. Tires are ready; the holdup is the charging-out process on Aurora Lane. Could ship today; awaiting Fred's resolution on how the tires get charged out.
  • Backup truck availability: When a truck goes down, there is not always a backup truck available — meaning either a load runs late or a truck is pulled out of service that shouldn't be. The team has to choose between truck-count metrics and reliable spares. There is a partial solution starting for internal/terminal locations (queue trucks); non-terminal locations still have no solution.
Opportunity — Sleeper Compartment as On-Truck Parts Bay

A specific Expedited advantage that is not being used today: under normal slip-seat operation, the sleeper compartment is empty. Drivers start and end at their home yard, so the bunk, storage cubbies, and sleeper space are not used for personal effects the way they would be on an OTR truck. That is a meaningful amount of secured, on-truck storage space sitting idle on every Expedited tractor.

  • The proposal: Stock the sleeper compartment with a small, standardized parts kit so that common road-side issues can be resolved by the driver without a service call, a tow, or a delay to the shuttle. The pigtail wedge already documented in T8 (Driver Mgmt & Pay) is the obvious starter item — a $7 part that solves a recurring lights/connection complaint — but the same logic extends to other low-cost, high-frequency items.
  • Candidate items for the kit (illustrative, to be finalized with maintenance): pigtail wedge, glad-hand seals and grommets, spare fuses, gladhand wrench, zip ties, a small assortment of trailer light bulbs, electrical tape, a flashlight, basic inspection mirror, a dedicated tire pressure gauge. The list should be short, light, and curated by maintenance — not an open-ended toolbox.
  • Why this is a good fit for Expedited specifically: the sleeper space is genuinely unused on these trucks; the routes are predictable so resupply is straightforward; the time cost of any roadside delay falls directly on the shuttle handoff (so the savings are concentrated, not diffuse); and the same kit can travel with the truck through a slip-seat swap without needing to be re-issued each shift.
  • Implementation requirements:
    1. Maintenance owns the parts list, the resupply cadence, and the inventory check. The kit should not become a junk drawer.
    2. Orientation needs to cover what is in the kit, when to use it (and when not to — the kit is for legitimate small fixes, not deferred maintenance), and the expectation that drivers report kit usage so the resupply works.
    3. The kit needs a dedicated, labeled location in the sleeper so a swapping driver can see at a glance what is on the truck.
    4. A short feedback loop — first 30 days — to see what items actually get used, what doesn’t, and what is missing.
  • What this could pay back: reduction in roadside service calls, fewer T-call cascades caused by minor truck issues, faster recovery on small problems that today turn into 30–60 minute delays. A pilot on one or two lanes (e.g., 147 and 148) for 60 days would size the impact before broader rollout.
Worth Revisiting — Sleeper Amenities (Mattress, Fridge, TV)

A related decision worth revisiting: maintenance was instructed to remove the mattress, refrigerator, and TV from Expedited tractors because of cleanliness and shared-equipment concerns when trucks rotate between drivers. The TV and fridge calls are defensible on their face — but the mattress decision is worth a second look.

  • The TV removal makes sense. A TV is non-essential, easy to break, and a clear shared-use friction point with no operational upside.
  • The fridge removal is reasonable. The likely concern — drivers leaving food behind, smells, mold, cleaning burden — is real and recurring. Defensible without much debate.
  • The mattress decision is worth reconsidering. The driving concern was bedbugs / lice / hygiene transferring between drivers. That concern is real, but two pieces of context deserve more weight than they got the first time around:
    1. The mattress is plastic-covered — the surface is wipeable and does not retain pests the way a fabric mattress would.
    2. If a driver wants to use the bunk for a quick rest on a layover or weather delay, bringing their own sheets is cheaper than a hotel and far more convenient than running off-route. The driver carries their own bedding the same way they carry their own clothes.
  • The hygiene argument also has a consistency problem. The same bedbug/lice concern that justified pulling the mattress applies just as directly to the driver’s seat, the steering wheel, the sleeper’s upholstered surfaces, and the seatbelt — none of which were removed (and obviously can’t be). If the seat is acceptable with a basic cleaning protocol, the plastic-covered mattress should be too.
  • Proposed protocol if the mattress is reinstated:
    1. Plastic cover stays on, no exceptions; replaced on a defined schedule.
    2. Drivers bring their own sheets and pillow — same as personal effects.
    3. Mandatory wipe-down at end of shift before slip-seat handoff, same as cab interior expectation.
    4. Orientation language is explicit on the protocol so the expectations are set up front.
  • Why this is worth the leadership conversation: the original decision was made for a reasonable concern, but the cost of the decision is paid every time a driver gets caught in a layover, a HOS reset, or a weather delay and has no on-truck rest option. For a slip-seat operation that is supposed to keep drivers home daily, the layover scenarios are the exception — but they happen, and the cost of a hotel + repositioning vs. a few minutes in a properly-protected bunk is meaningful over the year.
T11
Training, Standards & Leadership Development
SOP gaps, training depth, identified skill gaps, and team capability
What Was Observed
  • No SOPs exist as a reference for the team. There are no documented standard operating procedures covering planning, dispatch, EPM workflow, communication protocols, or exception handling.
  • Most team members received only two days of initial training — much of what they know is self-taught through trial and error.
  • Documentation that does exist is fragmented across other departments' libraries and not labeled "Expedited." To find a topic the team has to know which department's library to look in (e.g., "driver payroll," "customer requirements"). There is no consolidated, Expedited-labeled reference. Casey returning home post-walk illustrates the impact — questions that would have been answered in seconds in person now have to be asked one-by-one because there is no document to point him to.
  • Identified skill gaps: Regina, Alan, and Brian each need more one-on-one training in planning. Regina is reliant on inefficient communication channels. Tyler appears disengaged. Regina and Tyler are seen as lacking the recovery "know-how" — considered a training gap, not a hiring issue. (Freight-finding is named by every team member as the hardest part of the role; that discussion lives in T9.)
  • Cross-training across all lanes at the day center is needed — single-lane silos create fragility and dependency. "Fire hose" training methods noted as ineffective.
  • Joe starting Monday to train/support planning — particularly for Oklahoma–Tennessee and interlink groups. This is a positive step.
  • Initiative gap: A leadership observation — team members tend to come ask before trying to resolve a problem on their own. Some of that is the SOP gap (no documents to look in), but some is a confidence and decision-making gap. Best-practice guidance, not just procedures, would help — examples of "in this situation, here's how an experienced dispatcher thinks about it."
  • Morale is low due to duplicated and manual work, office return constraints, and unclear leadership expectations on lane profitability. Morale was observed to climb visibly during the walk as the team saw active engagement and questions being asked.
  • 7-on/7-off schedule is the primary reason many employees joined this group and remains popular. Confusion exists around PTO and overtime for salaried employees on this schedule. The "double-paying" question (PTO + covering a shift) was raised — agreed that salaried managers covering shifts does not qualify for overtime.
  • Orientation gap: Presenters need to communicate the "why" behind policies (e.g., the financial impact of a driver no-show). Suggestion that drivers arrive 1 hour early for thorough pre-trip inspections — this should be formally communicated in orientation.
  • Freight marking consumes ~2 hours per person per day across the teams. Much of that time is a direct consequence of the training gap described above — team members are doing the work out of survival rather than following a trained standard. Reducing this load requires a documented marking standard plus the training to apply it consistently.
  • SOP build approach: The plan is to do SOP build-out by sitting with the work — most likely with Davey — from an outsider's viewpoint, capturing exactly which screens to open and which steps to take. This avoids the "team writes their own SOP" trap (the work as imagined is not the work as done). The result should be usable by anyone new walking in cold.
KPIs & Accountability — The Measurement Gap

A finding that surfaced repeatedly across the walk: individual KPIs do not exist for most team members on the Expedited team. Performance is observed informally, coached anecdotally, and measured almost entirely at the lane or fleet level — not at the seat level. Without seat-level measurement, there is no objective way to hold team members accountable, no way to identify high performers vs. struggling performers early, and no clear path for a new dispatcher to know what "good" looks like.

  • The measurement gap is partly a tooling gap. Many of the activities that should be measured at the individual level — load assignments completed on time, missed handoffs, OOR miles authored by planner, T-call rate by dispatcher, response time on driver chats, freight marking accuracy — are either not captured anywhere, captured in ways that can’t be attributed to a person, or trapped inside the manual spreadsheet. The data exists in pieces; it is not stitched together at the seat level.
  • The measurement gap is also a definition gap. Even where data could be extracted, no one has defined what the seat-level KPIs should be. There is no agreed list of "these are the five things every dispatcher is measured on." That definition has to come before the tooling work, otherwise we automate the wrong things.
  • The accountability impact is real. Without KPIs, the team falls back on subjective evaluation — "Davey’s a strong night planner," "Tyler appears disengaged," "Regina is reliant on inefficient channels." Those observations are accurate, but they aren’t backed by numbers, which makes corrective coaching harder, makes the case for headcount or compensation changes harder, and makes it nearly impossible to defend a difficult conversation if it ever has to escalate.
  • What a starter set could look like (illustrative, not prescriptive): handoff-quality score from a structured handoff template (T7), planning lead-time vs. departure (T4 — Planning Workflow), driver chat response time, OOR miles authored, freight-marking accuracy, T-call rate by lane and dispatcher, attendance/no-show by lane the dispatcher covers, and SOP adherence on the standardized workflow once that exists.
  • Sequence: Define the KPI list first (1–2 weeks of leadership work). Then identify which ones can be tracked from existing data, which need spreadsheet/EPM changes, and which need new instrumentation. Then publish the dashboard and make it part of the weekly leadership review — not as a punitive tool, but as a coaching artifact and a fairness mechanism.
Training Success Story — Tyler, Regina & Amye (Highlight of the Week)
  • Starting state: At the beginning of the week, Tyler was functioning as Regina's assistant on Lane 147 rather than as an owner of any portion of the lane. This was largely a reflection of the lack of formal training — not a reflection of his capability.
  • Intervention: Mid-week, Amye was asked to spend direct, focused time with Tyler and enable him to own half of the lane. The coaching was deliberate, on-the-floor, and structured around him doing the work rather than being told the answer.
  • Outcome: The intervention worked. Tyler took ownership of his half of the lane, and Regina was able to leave her laptop at the office for the first time in weeks — a concrete, measurable indicator that the workload had finally been rebalanced.
  • Why this matters: This was the highlight moment of the week. It is direct evidence that the team's capability gaps respond quickly to focused, structured coaching. The intervention was low cost, but the impact on Regina's recovery and Tyler's engagement was immediate. This is the pattern that should be replicated across the other identified skill gaps (Regina on planning, Alan and Brian on planning, cross-training across lanes).
T12
Support Staff Schedule & Coverage Model
Current lane-level staffing, weekly coverage pattern, and management span implications
What Was Observed — Current Staffing Model
  • Expedited is organized into six primary lane groupings plus two pending additions, with each lane covered by a dayside team and a nightside team.
  • Two managers cover all lanes: Amye Alvarez (147 / 155) and Nathan Kratz (156 / 151 / 152 / 148 / 149 / 154) — a 6-lane span of control for one manager.
  • The default schedule is seven days on, twelve hours per day (Monday through Sunday) — roughly 84 scheduled hours per week per specialist.
  • Four specialists run non-default schedules: Krista Vokey and Sara Weltz work 4-on / 4-off; Bryan Ryder and Josh Catton split fixed weekdays with a rotating Fri / Sat / Sun.
  • David Klement and Mike Howell are designated Night Leads on 155 (OK / TN) and also cover lane 147 on nightside.
  • Jordan Fream and Cecil Watts each cover three lanes on nights (151 / 156 / 152) — a heavier span than the nightside 147 / 155 pairing.
Management Coverage
Manager
Amye AlvarezLanes 147 / 155
Nathan KratzLanes 156 / 151 / 152 / 148 / 149 / 154

Leadership: John Hessler (Director — Expedited Operations) and Lyndi Curry (Director — Expedited Operations) sit above the two managers.

Leadership Capacity & Staffing
  • Amye Alvarez (Driver Leader, Lane 147) has expressed intent to step down from her leadership role due to an ongoing family matter requiring her attention.
  • Overnight coverage currently relies on a rotating leadership model. In practice, Davy absorbs the majority of night questions regardless of whose rotation it is — the rotation does not reflect actual workload distribution.
  • Across the leadership team, the current pace is not sustainable. Leaders are visibly stretched, and without structural decisions on coverage and capacity, burnout is a near-term risk rather than a hypothetical.
★
Key Opportunity — David Klement (Davy)
Davy is the clearest example in Expedited where a coverage fix and a talent-development decision are the same decision. Formalizing his role solves the night-rotation problem and protects a high-leverage internal coach at the same time.
Current Role
Night Lead on lanes 147 and 155. In practice, absorbs the majority of night questions regardless of whose rotation it is — the leadership night rotation does not reflect actual workload distribution.
Why He Stands Out
Depth of operational knowledge, demonstrated ability to train drivers and team members, and credibility with the team. The division's most visible in-house coach.
Proposed Structural Move
Formalize the role he is already filling — move Davy to a dedicated night management shift (for example, 3 PM – 3 AM or 12 PM – 12 AM). This would give drivers consistent evening leadership, run the evening calls under a single owner, and release the rest of the stretched leadership team from rotation load.
Why This Matters for the Long Game
As the team grows, Davy's capacity to pull new hires up the learning curve — standard work, onboarding, coaching — is a lever the Expedited division should not lose. Any structural, staffing, or pay decisions made here should be treated as both a coverage fix and a talent-retention / development decision.
Risk Flag — Leadership Burnout
At the current pace, leadership burnout is a near-term risk. The combination of Amye’s intent to step down and an ongoing night rotation that doesn’t reflect real workload will accelerate the problem unless structural decisions on coverage and capacity are made soon.
