Leadership Call-Outs
⚠ Schedule & Management Span
The 7-on / 7-off, 12-hour pattern is both a hiring incentive and a management constraint.

Several specialists currently on the 7-day-on schedule accepted the role because of that schedule — the 7-on / 7-off rotation is a meaningful draw and a retention lever that leadership should not treat casually. At the same time, it makes the role harder to support from a management standpoint: on any given week, half the experienced bench may be off, handoffs must carry seven days of context, coaching and in-the-moment feedback windows are compressed, and return-from-rotation creates 2,000+ email triage loads before productive work begins (see T6). Any change to the schedule model needs to weigh retention risk against span-of-control, coaching cadence, and coverage continuity.

The unified Davy opportunity — covering both the dedicated-night-coverage proposal and his role as an internal coach / rising star — is documented in Leadership Capacity & Staffing above.

Open Questions
E1
Executive Summary
To be completed upon return — written for leadership, not ops
Assessment Lead
On-Site Dates
Report Date
Prepared For
Division
Overall Assessment
Purpose of This Assessment

Hirschbach’s Expedited Division is approaching a fleet-size step change — a target of 200 trucks by September 2026, including 75 autonomous — and the operating model needs to be sound before that growth lands. The purpose of this assessment was to spend a structured week on the floor in Sapulpa, observe how the operation actually runs (versus how it is described on paper), and surface the gaps leadership needs to decide on before scaling. This document is the executive-level read-out of that walk and is meant to support a leadership decision on the strategic direction and immediate countermeasures for Expedited.

What We Found — Current State in Plain Language

The Expedited team is competent and working hard. The issues surfaced during the walk are not execution failures — they are structural and strategic.

  • The operating model has drifted. Expedited was designed as a self-contained slip-seat relay — drivers home daily, drop-and-hook, take-or-pay freight, no OTR support. Today, ~94% of orders on key cost centers depend on non-EXP trucks for pickup and/or delivery. Only ~6% of current freight fits the original self-contained model. Driscoll’s, at ~6% non-EXP dependency, is the one lane that runs essentially as the model intended — the exception that proves the rule. The drift also shows up in route efficiency: excluding Driscoll’s loads from the analysis, Expedited averages 113 out-of-route miles per order, compared to an average of 9 OOR miles on non-Expedited loads. The single biggest contributor is Walmart freight moving California → Missouri; outside that lane, the rest of the Expedited cost centers average ~100 OOR miles per order, with Memphis → Sapulpa being the worst at 128 miles. At ~700 orders per week running through Expedited, ~100 extra miles per order works out to roughly 70,000 additional miles per week being added by routing through Expedited rather than OTR.
  • The strategy is not aligned. The network operates as a mix of Models A, B, and C with no single agreed approach. The team described the network as an "octopus", expanding in many directions while priorities continue to shift.
  • The system of record is still a spreadsheet. EPM has been live for ~11 months and was scoped to replace the shared Excel file. It has not. The one-way sync to AS400, the "0022" dummy-appointment workaround, the absence of trailer data in EPM, and the T-Call cascade behavior (which silently clears OTR T-Calls when an Expedited leg is removed) all keep the manual spreadsheet as the day-to-day source of truth.
  • The handoffs are inconsistent. Two real handoff examples captured during the walk show the same operation handed off in radically different formats — a Saturday-evening freeform chat note on Lane 147, and a Friday email organized driver-by-driver on Lanes 151/156. Neither handoff is wrong on its own, but the variance between them is itself the problem: the receiving shift cannot build a quick-scan habit when every handoff is laid out differently.
  • SOPs and training are minimal. No documented standard operating procedures exist for the team. Most members received ~2 days of initial training; the rest is self-taught. Documentation that exists lives in other departments’ libraries, not labeled "Expedited" — making it hard to find and impossible to point a new hire to.
  • KPIs do not exist at the seat level. Performance is measured at the lane and fleet level, not the individual. There is no agreed list of dispatcher-level KPIs and the data needed to support them is in pieces, much of it trapped in the manual spreadsheet. This makes coaching subjective, makes accountability difficult to defend, and makes it hard to identify high or struggling performers early.
  • Planning is mostly coordination, not freight finding. Because ~94% of orders depend on non-EXP trucks, roughly 80% of the planner’s job is coordinating OTR pickups, monitoring deliveries, and reworking the plan mid-stream. Finding the load itself is the smaller part of the work.
  • Service performance has been under sustained pressure since December 2025 and has not fully recovered. This timing aligns with rapid Expedited growth — both in load volume and fleet size — but the relationship is not yet fully understood. We continue to see inconsistent on-time performance on T-called loads, particularly within Expedited; these loads are frequently awarded into areas with limited or misaligned capacity, creating downstream challenges for planners and increasing risk to service. While Expedited load volume has increased significantly over the past year and the fleet has expanded from 46 trucks to more than 60, it remains unclear whether service gaps are being driven by volume growth itself, capacity placement, or planning execution — raising a critical question: are service-related failures increasingly tied to planning errors as T-called volume grows in constrained markets?
  • The Operating Ratio is not telling the truth about Expedited's full cost. The OR as currently calculated for Expedited does not account for the first/final-mile cost when those legs are run by OTR — that cost is absorbed elsewhere on the P&L. Expedited dispatch and leadership headcount are not in the Expedited number. Equipment running in Expedited is being depreciated at the same rate as OTR despite running roughly twice the miles, which understates the true wear-and-tear cost being booked to the division. These are illustrative, not exhaustive — multiple cost categories are landing somewhere other than where they are incurred. Until the OR is rebuilt with full cost properly allocated, lane-level and division-level decisions are being made against a number that systematically understates Expedited's true operating cost — and lanes that look profitable today may not be once the full cost is on the page. The operation needs to be reassessed against a fully-loaded OR before further growth, lane, or investment decisions are anchored to the current figure.
  • Driver hiring is not aligned with the Elite Fleet standard. During Expedited orientation, an incoming driver assigned to the Expedited fleet required Foundations training and additional hands-on coaching due to identified skill gaps following time away from the truck. Orientation leadership aligned on allowing the driver to proceed conditionally, with plans to reassess performance after remedial training rather than making a qualification decision at the point of entry. This highlights a gap in the quality and readiness of drivers entering the Expedited pipeline: despite Expedited being positioned as the Elite / Blue Angel fleet, drivers are entering the operation without consistently meeting those expectations at hire. Indicators such as higher-than-normal call-ins, uneven experience levels, and increased need for post-hire remediation are placing strain on the operation. The current process permits drivers who do not clearly meet elite standards at entry to advance into Expedited, relying on training and reassessment to close gaps rather than ensuring alignment with the Elite / Blue Angel standard upfront.
  • Morale and retention are real, near-term risks. Morale was visibly low at the start of the week and rebounded during the walk under direct engagement. The 7-on/7-off salaried specialists accepted those roles in part for the schedule; current WFH/onsite-policy tension and management-time spent on manual driver pay (~25% of leadership time) are pressure points worth treating seriously. Compounding the schedule pressure, during the 90-day sprint window team members effectively cannot take time off without leadership stepping in to cover the seat or arranging a swap with another team member — the bench depth is not there to absorb a planned absence without either incurring extra coverage cost or pulling management onto the floor. The PTO model and the coverage model are not aligned, and the seat-level fragility is being absorbed by leadership rather than solved structurally.
The Core Gap

This is a strategic alignment gap, not a tactical execution gap. There is no shared answer to two foundational questions — what Expedited is, and why we are doing it. Until those are answered, every downstream decision (which lanes to keep, who to hire, what to bid, how to measure performance, what to invest in) is being made against a different mental model. The reframe that holds up against everything observed is: Expedited is not "moving faster freight" — it is moving freight differently, on the crawl-walk-run path to autonomous, in exchange for committed customer volume. That framing aligns the operating model with where the company is going and gives Sales a credible reason to revisit closed bids mid-cycle.

Top Constraints Confirmed On-Site
  1. Strategic ambiguity. No agreed answer to "what is Expedited, why are we doing it." Symptom: lanes operating against different models, sales bidding against current capability rather than future positioning, drivers and team unable to articulate the value proposition.
  2. Demand gap on most lanes. ~94% of orders depend on non-EXP trucks. Lanes without committed take-or-pay volume require freight to be borrowed from other lanes or divisions, which masks the underlying demand gap and makes the OR look better than it is once costs are properly allocated.
  3. EPM as system of record is structurally incomplete. One-way sync to AS400, dummy-appointment workaround, no trailer data, T-Call cascade that silently clears OTR T-Calls. The team cannot run the operation in EPM as currently configured — and after 11 months that pattern is settling in.
  4. KPI and measurement gap. No seat-level KPIs are defined or tracked. Tooling gap (data is in pieces) compounds a definition gap (no agreed list of what dispatchers are measured on).
  5. Training and SOP gap. No documented SOPs for planning, dispatch, EPM workflow, communication protocols, or exception handling. Single-lane silos and "fire-hose" training methods are creating fragility that scales poorly.
Immediate Risks (Before A3 Countermeasures)
  • Service degradation as volume grows. Load count is up ~6× year-over-year while seated truck count has not kept pace. Without the SOPs, KPIs, and tooling fixes, on-time and lane-failure metrics will move in the wrong direction faster than headcount can absorb.
  • Silent OTR-side handoff failures from the T-Call cascade. The current EPM behavior clears OTR T-Calls when an Expedited leg is removed, with no notification to the OTR planner. The risk is missed handoffs, incorrect routing, or a driver continuing past an intended drop — with the consequence borne by people who didn’t make the change.
  • Retention exposure on the 7-on/7-off bench. The schedule is the primary reason several specialists accepted the role. Combined with the current WFH-policy tension and the cumulative fatigue load of the seven-day stretch, the retention risk is concrete — and the bench depth is thin enough that one or two departures meaningfully degrade coverage.
  • Continued OR distortion. Expedited’s OR appears favorable in part because pickup, delivery, T-call assist, and borrowed freight costs are absorbed by OTR. Continuing on this trajectory makes it harder to evaluate which lanes actually deserve growth investment versus which are quietly subsidized.
  • "Elite Fleet" language outpacing hiring criteria. Orientation positions Expedited drivers as a premium tier, but current screening criteria are baseline. The credibility gap shows up first in driver retention and second in customer conversations where the premium-fleet pitch needs to be defensible.
Recommended Next Steps

In sequence — later items depend on the earlier ones being settled:

  1. Leadership alignment session on the two foundational questions (1–2 weeks). What is Expedited? Why are we doing it? Without a shared answer, the rest of this list is premature. Output: a written value proposition usable in customer conversations and in driver orientation.
  2. Lane classification. Formal decision on Model A target lanes (autonomous-readiness pathway) vs. Model B/C support lanes, with Sales aligned on the customer-target list. Lanes without committed volume should be evaluated against the OTR baseline, not kept on Expedited "to fill capacity."
  3. Define and instrument seat-level KPIs. 1–2 weeks of leadership work to define the list (handoff-quality score, planning lead-time, driver chat response time, OOR by planner, freight-marking accuracy, T-call rate by lane and dispatcher, attendance by lane). Then identify which can be tracked from existing data, which need EPM/spreadsheet changes, and which need new instrumentation. Publish as a weekly dashboard framed as a coaching artifact, not a punitive tool.
  4. SOP build — sit with the work. Build SOPs with Davey from an outsider’s viewpoint, capturing exact screens and steps. Avoid the "team writes their own SOP" trap. 60–90 days; output is a usable reference that a new hire can follow cold.
  5. Run targeted pilots. Each of these is small, reversible, and will size impact before any broader commitment: $5/week attendance incentive on a single lane for 90 days; sleeper parts kit on Lanes 147 and 148 for 60 days; reinstated mattress with documented protocol on a pilot lane.
  6. Customer-side reframe. Equip Sales with the autonomous-readiness pitch (committed volume in exchange for early autonomous deployment) and identify the first three target customers. This unlocks the demand side of the model that the operating side has been trying to compensate for.
  7. Address the EPM T-Call cascade. Engage the EPM vendor on a confirmation dialog, soft-delete / "hold for replanning" mode, and OTR-planner notification when cross-division T-Calls are about to clear. This is the highest-risk technical issue surfaced and should not wait for a broader EPM rework.
What We Did Not Investigate (Scope Boundaries)

The walk was deliberately scoped to on-floor observation of Expedited dispatch operations. The following are out of scope for this report and may warrant their own structured investigations:

  • Customer interviews and contract review — the value-proposition reframe needs to be tested with the actual target customers (Anheuser-Busch, Pepsi, Walmart suppliers, etc.) before it is committed to.
  • Financial restatement and OR allocation. The observation that Expedited’s OR is distorted by OTR-absorbed costs is directional; a formal restatement (with FP&A) is needed before lane-level investment decisions are made.
  • EPM technical deep-dive with the vendor. The findings here are from the user side. A vendor-side review of integration architecture, T-Call behavior, and roadmap timing should follow.
  • Recruiting funnel and hiring profile. The "Elite Fleet language vs. hiring criteria" gap was observed but not solved — what an Elite Fleet driver profile actually looks like, and whether the current funnel selects for it, is its own piece of work.
  • Detailed safety review. Crash counts and preventability were reviewed at a summary level. A deeper preventability and root-cause analysis of the 9 preventable crashes in the 13-week window would be a natural follow-on.
  • Autonomous-readiness operational requirements. If autonomous lanes are the strategic anchor, the operational and compliance prerequisites for autonomous deployment on a chosen lane need to be mapped end-to-end.

Constraint Validation
Hypotheses — not conclusions
Constraint Hypothesis
Observed?
Evidence / Notes
Freight suitability — loads in the lanes don't match the relay model requirements
Driver profile misalignment — who we hire vs. what the operation requires
Equipment reliability — older trucks failing at higher-than-modeled rates
Technology gaps — EPM, route lines, planning module creating manual workaround burden
Single decision-maker bottleneck — operation can't scale beyond current state
Handoff breakdown — AM/PM shift change losing critical load information
Profitability model distorted — depreciation not adjusted for actual EXP mileage
Additional constraints observed — not on the hypothesis list

Key Documents & References
  • EXP Freight Master.xlsx — lane-by-lane freight sourcing guide; customer and PU/DELV locations per lane
  • Weekly EXP KPI Summary.xlsm — utilization, turnover, crash, DH, broker freight data (13-wk rolling)
  • Expedited Miles, Trucks, Utilization.xlsm — daily/weekly pivot data; seated vs. unseated trucks
  • Northern EXP Lanes Daily Load List.xlsx — manual daily tracking spreadsheet; one tab per lane
  • Active Trucks in EXP Cost Centers.xlsm — current truck assignment and status
  • SOP 147 — Expedited Southern Lane Relay Operations (trailer swap protocol, cleanliness, scale tickets)
  • SOP 8.20.25 — Full Truck Swap Slip Seat Relay Operations
  • SOP 086 — Expedited Communication ROAD (breakdown escalation; Road Assist: 402-404-2035)
  • Expedited Operation Driver Expectations (service failure framework; scale ticket policy)
  • Expedited Tablet Log-Out Process.docx
  • EXP Crash / Cargo Details Last Week.xlsm
  • Expedited Daily Briefing and Debriefing.xlsx
  • Expedited Implementation Plan Master.xlsb — WIP, working document
